experiment: F-HS1 compaction deficit: swarm as bureaucracy self-test
method: Measure compaction rate across 3 knowledge tiers (lessons, principles, tools) using git history + SUPERSEDED DAG + archival records. Compute production:compaction ratio per tier. Test whether operational proximity predicts compaction rate.
expect: Swarm compaction deficit ~82:1. proxy-K rise correlates with lesson accumulation. Compaction covers <5% of production.

lessons.mechanism: SUPERSEDED (L->L horizontal revision 38%, L->P vertical promotion 62%)

tools.mechanism: Binary utility test — unused tools archived after DOMEX lane merges (L-644)

tier_gradient.finding: Items closer to action (tools) compact at 20x the rate of items closer to knowledge (lessons). Operational proximity predicts compaction rate.
tier_gradient.correlation: tier_operational_proximity vs compaction_rate: monotonic across 3 tiers
tier_gradient.mechanism_hypothesis: Tools have binary utility (works/doesn't work) — natural selection pressure. Lessons have no usage test — survive by inertia. Principles sit between: abstract enough to accumulate, but periodically audited (dedup passes).

bureaucratic_comparison.shared_failure_mode: Both accumulate declarative knowledge monotonically. Compaction mechanisms exist but operate at <3% of production rate. Growth is structural (new knowledge creates dependencies that resist deletion).
bureaucratic_comparison.key_difference: Swarm has explicit compaction mechanism (compact.py, SUPERSEDED, proxy-K tracking). Bureaucracies rely on sunset clauses which are rarely mandatory. But having the mechanism doesn't prevent the deficit — 82:1 production:compaction at lesson tier.
bureaucratic_comparison.f_hs1_answer: Bureaucracy loses compaction ability at the declarative tier — where knowledge has no usage-based selection pressure. The same gradient exists in the swarm: tools (operational) compact at 55%, lessons (declarative) at 2.7%. The mechanism is identical: operational items face binary fitness tests (works/fails); declarative items survive by citation inertia.

expect_deficit_82_1.actual: 37:1 (lessons only, including dedupes) or 82:1 (SUPERSEDED-only per L-684 methodology)
expect_deficit_82_1.verdict: CONFIRMED at SUPERSEDED level. Lower at all-mechanism level.

expect_proxy_k_correlates.prediction: proxy-K rise correlates with lesson accumulation
expect_proxy_k_correlates.actual: proxy-K grew 2.68x over 273 sessions while lessons grew from ~100 to 636 (6.36x). proxy-K grows SLOWER than lesson count — compaction partially works at tier level
expect_proxy_k_correlates.verdict: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED — growth correlated but sub-linear

expect_sub_5pct.actual: lesson tier: 2.7% (CONFIRMED). principle tier: 12.3% (above 5%). tool tier: 55% (far above). Overall: tier-dependent.
expect_sub_5pct.verdict: CONFIRMED for lessons; FALSIFIED for principles and tools

actual: Three-tier compaction gradient: tools 55% > principles 12.3% > lessons 2.7%. Operational proximity predicts compaction rate (monotonic). proxy-K sub-linear vs lesson count (compaction partially effective). F-HS1 answered: declarative knowledge resists compaction because it lacks usage-based selection pressure. Same mechanism in bureaucracies.
diff: Predicted ~82:1 uniform deficit — got tier-dependent (1.8:1 to 37:1). Did NOT predict the operational-declarative gradient (main finding). proxy-K prediction partially confirmed (sub-linear growth, not linear). <5% prediction confirmed for lessons only, falsified for upper tiers.
